title: Trump's Taiwan Calculus, DeepSeek's State-Backed Billions, and Iran's Gas Station Cyberattacks
subhead: The US President frames Taiwan arms sales as a 'negotiating chip' with Beijing, China's state AI fund bets $4B on DeepSeek, and Iranian hackers breach US fuel infrastructure as geopolitical tensions escalate.

sections.heading: Trump Reframes Taiwan as a 'Negotiating Chip' With China
sections.body:
U.S. President Donald Trump described potential arms sales to Taiwan as a 'very good negotiating chip' in dealings with China, heightening anxieties in Taipei about American commitment to the island's defense. In a Fox News interview following his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, Trump said he was holding a $14 billion arms package 'in abeyance' and that its fate 'depends on China.'

The comments came after Xi warned Trump of 'clashes and even conflicts' if the Taiwan issue is mishandled — one of Beijing's starkest statements to date. Taiwan's presidential office sought to smooth tensions, saying U.S. policy 'remains unchanged,' while the $11 billion arms package approved in December remains in place.

Trump also pressed for Taiwan's semiconductor industry to relocate to the U.S., telling Fox News he wanted 'everybody making chips over in Taiwan to come into America.' Taiwan has committed $250 billion in U.S. chip sector investments, including TSMC's $165 billion Arizona mega-campus. The president repeated claims that Taiwan 'stole' its chipmaking sector from the U.S. decades ago.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Taiwan 'did not feature primarily' in the Beijing summit discussions, and the White House readout made no mention of the island. Trump told Taiwan and China to 'both cool it' and expressed opposition to fighting a war '9,500 miles away.'
sections.whyItMatters: For decades, U.S. policy treated Taiwan as a fixed commitment under the Taiwan Relations Act. Trump is explicitly recasting it as leverage in trade and geopolitical negotiations — a shift that could accelerate Beijing's timeline for coercion and force Taipei to reassess its defense posture.

sections.heading: DeepSeek Secures Up to $4 Billion in First External Round, Backed by China's State Funds
sections.body:
Chinese AI lab DeepSeek is finalizing its first external funding round targeting up to $4 billion at a $50 billion valuation — a fivefold jump from early April estimates. The lead investor is China's National AI Industry Investment Fund, an $8.8 billion state-backed vehicle, with the semiconductor-focused Big Fund III also participating alongside Tencent.

The funding comes three weeks after DeepSeek released V4, a 1.6-trillion-parameter model designed to dramatically cut memory and compute costs for long-context AI. DeepSeek claims V4-Pro needs only 27% of the per-token inference compute and 10% of the KV-cache memory of its predecessor at a one-million-token context window.

DeepSeek gave Huawei early access to optimize V4 while denying Nvidia and AMD the same courtesy. Huawei's Ascend 950 chips now support V4 for inference, and ByteDance, Tencent, and Alibaba have rushed orders for Huawei silicon. NIST's evaluation puts V4-Pro about eight months behind leading U.S. models — a gap DeepSeek disputes.

The U.S. House Select Committee on China has called DeepSeek 'a profound threat to our nation's security,' citing potential export control violations and data security risks. Security researchers found a publicly accessible database containing over one million user records, including chat histories and API keys. Italy has banned DeepSeek under GDPR, with investigations underway across multiple EU countries.
sections.whyItMatters: China's state funds are co-signing a bet that efficient, open-weight models can challenge U.S. AI dominance while running on domestic silicon. The investment signals Beijing views frontier AI and semiconductor self-sufficiency as a single strategic problem.

sections.heading: Iran-Linked Hackers Breach US Gas Station Fuel Monitoring Systems
sections.body:
U.S. officials suspect Iranian hackers are behind a series of breaches of automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems at gas stations across multiple states. The hackers exploited systems that were online and unprotected by passwords, allowing them to alter display readings — though not actual fuel levels.

The breaches raise safety concerns because ATG access could theoretically allow attackers to mask gas leaks. CNN reports the campaign is the latest case of Tehran targeting U.S. critical infrastructure amid the ongoing war. Previous Iranian-linked operations have hit U.S. water utilities, oil and gas sites, and medical device maker Stryker, and leaked the private emails of FBI Director Kash Patel.

Security researchers have warned about internet-facing ATGs for over a decade. A 2021 Sky News report cited IRGC documents singling out ATGs as potential targets for disruptive cyberattacks on gas stations. The hackers have used 'hacktivist' personas on Telegram to exaggerate exploits and publish stolen material.

Iran's cyber operations show 'significant increase in scale, speed, and integration' between cyber attacks and psychological campaigns, according to Israel's National Cyber Directorate. Allison Wikoff of PwC noted Iran is deploying 'good-enough malware' faster, including destructive wipers, alongside hack-and-leak operations against media and critical infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's wartime cyber campaign is opportunistic but persistent, targeting low-security infrastructure while U.S. officials warn of potential election interference ahead of midterms. The ATG breaches highlight a persistent gap in U.S. critical infrastructure security.

sections.heading: Vietnam's Critical Minerals Strategy Emerges From Japan Summit
sections.body:
Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae's visit to Hanoi this month revealed Vietnam's strategy to apply 'strategic autonomy' to critical minerals. The two countries signed six cooperation documents and identified rare earths, semiconductors, AI, and space as priority areas for bilateral cooperation.

Vietnam holds the world's sixth-largest rare earth reserves but was absent from the U.S. Critical Minerals Ministerial in February, where 55 partner countries convened. Hanoi's strategy is to keep decision-making independent while diversifying partnerships — engaging Washington and Beijing selectively while pursuing Japanese, Russian, and South Korean partners for capital and technology.

Vietnam's amended Law on Geology and Minerals, effective January 2026, classifies rare earths as a 'special strategic mineral,' bans export of unprocessed ore, and restricts mining rights to state-designated enterprises. The legal changes follow a crackdown on illegal extraction — including the arrest of 14 people linked to 291,000 tons of illegally extracted rare earth and iron ore, some smuggled to China.

China processes 85-100% of global rare earth refining and imposed export licensing on seven heavy rare earth elements in April 2025, forcing U.S. and European automakers to cut production. The U.S. has responded with Project Vault, a strategic stockpile, and 13 new bilateral mineral agreements.
sections.whyItMatters: Vietnam is positioning itself as a non-aligned alternative to China for critical minerals, but faces constraints: limited domestic processing capacity, high industrial electricity costs, and dependence on Chinese battery technology for its own EV industry.

sections.heading: Cerebras Soars in Blockbuster AI Chip IPO
sections.body:
Cerebras Systems made one of the biggest tech IPO debuts in history, closing its first trading day with a market cap near $100 billion — a level reached only by companies like Meta and Alibaba. The stock gave back 10% on Friday but remains a milestone for AI chip challengers.

The Silicon Valley company, founded in 2016, builds dinner-plate-sized chips designed for AI inference. Its WSE-3 chip is 57 times larger than the largest GPU and has 50 times the transistors. Unlike Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs, Cerebras chips are custom ASICs optimized for specific AI workloads — competing with Google's TPUs, Amazon's Trainium, and Groq's LPUs.

Cerebras now operates chips in its own data centers as a cloud service, with a $20 billion deal with OpenAI expiring in 2028. AWS announced in March it's using Cerebras chips. CFO Bob Komin said demand for fast inference is so high the company is 'sold out into 2027.'

Co-founders Andrew Feldman and Sean Lie became billionaires on Thursday. Nvidia acquired Cerebras competitor Groq for $20 billion in December, signaling the strategic value of inference-optimized silicon.
sections.whyItMatters: Cerebras' debut shows investor appetite for Nvidia alternatives is voracious. The IPO paves the way for other custom ASIC startups like Rebellions and validates the inference-first chip thesis as AI workloads shift from training to deployment.

sections.heading: SpaceX Targets May 19 for Starship V3 Debut Flight
sections.body:
SpaceX is targeting May 19 for the first launch of Starship Version 3, a roughly 408-foot rocket designed to deploy 22 satellites on its maiden flight. The launch from Starbase, Texas, marks the 12th Starship flight and the debut of the V3 architecture.

The test comes amid reports that SpaceX shareholders approved a 5-for-1 stock split ahead of a potential IPO. Bloomberg reports the company continues to dominate global launch capacity while developing Starship for lunar and Martian missions.

SpaceX is also planning to bring Starship operations to Florida, expanding beyond its Texas test site. The V3 vehicle represents a significant leap from earlier Starship iterations, with enhanced payload capacity for satellite deployment.
sections.whyItMatters: Starship V3's successful launch would cement SpaceX's position in heavy-lift launch for years, enabling larger satellite constellations and supporting U.S. space dominance as China advances its own launch capabilities.

sections.heading: Anthropic Warns US-China AI Gap Could Narrow Without Stronger Export Controls
sections.body:
Anthropic published a policy paper during Trump's China visit arguing that democracies must lead AI development or cede norm-setting to authoritarian regimes. The company estimates the U.S. currently has a 12-24 month lead in frontier AI, but warns that lead could erode without tighter chip export controls.

According to Anthropic's analysis, Huawei will produce only 4% of Nvidia's AI compute capability in 2026 and 2% in 2027, meaning China cannot bridge the compute gap alone. But two workarounds persist: smuggled Nvidia chips and distillation attacks — training Chinese models on outputs from U.S. frontier models.

Anthropic alleged DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax created over 24,000 fraudulent accounts and generated more than 16 million Claude interactions in violation of terms of service — a technique called distillation. DeepSeek has not publicly responded.

The paper outlines two 2028 scenarios: democratic AI dominance with sustained export controls and anti-distillation measures, or a 'neck-and-neck race' where Huawei and Alibaba expand global AI market share, particularly in the Global South.
sections.whyItMatters: Anthropic is lobbying for tighter enforcement of existing controls while competing Chinese models face both hardware constraints and accusations of intellectual extraction. The policy fight over AI chips and model access is intensifying as both sides claim the other is cheating.

sections.heading: Malta Becomes First Country to Give All Citizens ChatGPT Plus Access
sections.body:
OpenAI signed a world-first partnership with Malta to provide all citizens free access to ChatGPT Plus for one year after completing a government-backed AI literacy course developed by the University of Malta.

The program, managed by the Malta Digital Innovation Authority, launches this month. Citizens and residents registered with Malta's online identity system are eligible. Economy Minister Silvio Schembri said the goal is to turn AI 'from an unfamiliar concept into practical assistance.'

The deal is part of OpenAI's 'OpenAI for Countries' initiative, which has partnered with Estonia for secondary school ChatGPT access and Greece for government applications. OpenAI has also struck a deal with the U.S. Department of Defense to deploy models on classified military networks.
sections.whyItMatters: Malta is the first country to treat ChatGPT as a public utility, signaling how governments may integrate AI access into digital citizenship programs. The model could spread to other small nations seeking to upskill populations without domestic AI infrastructure.

bottomLine: Trump's transactional framing of Taiwan arms sales — holding a $14B package 'in abeyance' as negotiating leverage — marks a significant shift from decades of U.S. policy that treated the island's defense as a fixed commitment under the Taiwan Relations Act. The same week, China's state funds poured up to $4B into DeepSeek, betting that efficient open-weight models running on domestic silicon can challenge U.S. AI dominance. Meanwhile, Iranian hackers exploited password-free fuel monitoring systems at U.S. gas stations — a low-tech vulnerability in critical infrastructure that underscores how persistent, not sophisticated, threats remain a gap in U.S. cybersecurity posture.
sources: CNBC, CNN, Reuters, The Diplomat, TechTimes, Cointelegraph, Digit, ABC17 News, Associated Press, NBC News, Fox News, Atlantic Council, International Crisis Group, NIST
